speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November the 28th, 1995. The survivor is Mrs. Sonia Tessler, T-E-S-S-L-E-R. Maiden name, Awret, which is A-W-R-E-T. Interviewer, Felicia Carmelly, C-A-R-M-E-L-L-Y. We are in Toronto, Canada. This interview is in English. Hello, my name is Felicia Carmelly. Today is November the 28th, 1995. I will be interviewing Mrs. Sonia Tessler, maiden name, Awret. This interview is in English. We are in Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sonia Tessler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name is Awret. I was born in Korzec, on the border of Poland and Russia. We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: But the country-- but it wasn't Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The border of Poland and Russia. I was-- my--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year were you born? What's your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1921, The 1st of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were nine children. I was the baby. My father's name was Avram Awret. My mother's name was Riva Awret. My oldest sister was Ruchel. The second was Esther, Sylvia, Tzipora, Yacov. And another brother, [? Meier, ?] which the Russians killed in 1917, the Bolsheviks. And then, I had another sister Tzipora, Chava, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was, at that time, a wealthy man. We had a big store of yard goods. But in 1917, the Bolsheviks came to Poland. Because it was coming in [INAUDIBLE]. It was times when they came. And it-- Poland, Russia, Poland, Poland, Russia. And then, they took away everything. They took carpets and they cut it for their horses. And then, I wasn't born at that time. But this is all the stories what my parents told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from your life as a little girl at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had very-- a very happy life. But we were, at that time when I was born, we were poor. But otherwise, we were-- I remember the-- the home. My father was very Orthodox. The whole family was Orthodox. And the Shabbat was wonderful, even if it wasn't too much to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday, 12 o'clock, the children have to get washed and dressed. The table was all white in the candlelight. It-- that's beautiful. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, take your time. What else do you remember from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I grew up, the situation was very bad, when it came to money and to survive. But I had two sisters where they left Korzec a long time ago. And they helped us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it must be about '29, '28. Not exactly I don't remember. And during the war, this was my aim, to come to see my sister. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Take your time. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I knew my whole family's gone. And this kept me alive. Whatever happened, I had the strength to live and to meet my sisters, which I did. And I'm very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the last holiday you celebrated at home with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- we celebrate all the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last holiday at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last holiday, my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I left, my father died. And I was about 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember exactly. They are so mixed up in the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we still celebrated Pesach and Rosh Hashana, Yom Kippur, all the holidays-- very Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so what-- do you remember-- who did you play with when you were a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children, with the neighbors going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family live in a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a Jewish neighborhood. And I always-- and with-- my friends, which they had grandparents. And my best friend, every Saturday, went to visit their grandparents. And I felt like I would like to have grandparents. So every Saturday, I went to visit her grandparents. And I adopted-- they adopted me, like a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They died. Because when I was born, I was born at the elder-- old age for my parents. So they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never had-- I never met my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I'm talking to my grandchildren, telling them how lucky they are by having a grandmother and a grandfather, which I missed in my youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So when-- when were you first aware that there is anti-Semitism? That things are not so good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in school. In school, we met them every time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it was Easter time, and we-- our house-- across our house was a Ukranian church. And when it was Easter, we had to stay home and close the shutters, close the doors. Because it was-- they tore the fences and they made fire. And if we would go out outside, for sure we would be killed. So we had to stay home over the holidays-- Ukrainian holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, many times, they said we are dirty Jew. And we fought. We were fighting as much as we could. If my best friends heard they are dirty Jew, they start a fight. That's the way we grew up. And when we saw a police-- a policeman, we ran away, even we didn't do nothing. I remember when it come Saturday morning, we used to go and play near the river. It was a little river. And it was a beautiful view. And all of a sudden, the Polish police with their big, nice horses came out. And we had to run away to hide. Because if they saw somebody, they beat them half to death. So as soon we saw, we ran away and we were hiding. Until-- even when I came to Canada, when I saw a policeman, my heart was beating just like I did something wrong. Until today, I still can go over all the things that I've been through my youth. When I got the citizen papers after being five years in Canada and the [INAUDIBLE] came to take information, I cried. And I couldn't talk, because I was scared. And the men-- the mounted police said, Mrs. Tessler, quiet down. I didn't come to arrest you. I just-- you're going-- you are in Canada. You're not in Europe. It took me a while until I quiet down. This was my youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, in 1939, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hitler made a pact with Stalin. And they took Poland. And they divided in two parts. Up to the River Bug belonged to Russia. And from the River Bug belonged to the Germans. And they were from 1939 to 1941. And in 1941, of course, Hitler broke the pact. And-- and they-- they went after Russia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1939 up to 1941, I couldn't go to school. I couldn't study. I couldn't work. Because they said that I'm a capitalist's daughter, which they were in 1970, not when I was born. But it didn't matter. They didn't let me to do nothing. Then I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the Communists that said that about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Russians from 1939 to 1941. So I tried to belong to a group-- a youth group. They call it Komsomols. And from the Komsomols, from the youth, you're gonna be a real-- a real Communist, which I didn't do it. Because I liked the Communists just to survive and to get the job or to go to school, whatever. All the doors, everything was closed for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went twice. They all rejected me, even my friends, which I went to school together, Jewish friends. They said that I'm from a very fine family. I kept my nose high. Because we were the top of our town. And they think that I shouldn't belong to this group. And the third time I went-- which my mother insisted I should go-- and-- they accepted me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about a few months before the Germans broke the pact with Russia. And I knew that I had to leave. Because the first what Hitler comes, they killed the youth. And then, Hitler was already there in [? the Rovna, ?] which is close to Korzec. It's about two hours by car. The whole family got together, and they decided that I should go over-- which it was the border. It was no man's land for many years between Poland and Russia-- but now it's open. And I should go and have a look what's doing there. And maybe, the whole family can come with us. I packed a few things. And my nephew, he went with me too. Because he belonged to the group too. And we left--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Komsomol, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Komsomol.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 18. And-- and-- they-- what was your nephew's name? Gordon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left. I took a few things in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rucksack.

speaker: interviewee
text: --rucksack, with the idea that I'm coming back to tell what was going on. We walked a whole day. And then, it got dark. We went into a forest, a deep forest, because the bombs were flying. And it was dark. We couldn't go no more. In the forest, there was screaming, mother slash children. And everybody didn't know where they were going. I couldn't wait till it got a little bit light.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: All refugees what they running-- run away from-- from Germany, from Poland. They're hiding. And I came to the highway, and I wanted to go back home. Russian soldiers were staying with ammunition. They didn't let us go back. They said, if you're going back, they'll kill us on the spot. Because we are German spies. I cried. It hurt me. I begged them. I did-- I couldn't go back. That was the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the end of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of seeing my family. And we decided we have to go farther.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Gordon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gordon, yeah. I was all the time with him. And we decided we have to leave. We're not going to go. And we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Further east, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Farther to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Deep to Russia. And I'll tell you, we were one step ahead of Hitler. If he just took away Korzec, we went to Nowogrod Bobrzanski. He took Nowogrod Bobrzanski, we went to Kiev, then Kharkov, Poltava. And finally, we finish up in Stalingrad. We are so far--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalingrad. And he will not get us. Because Russia is strong. Russia will win.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a question. What did you eat? And where did you sleep? And how long was this trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked, we drove on donkeys and horses and train and any place we could. We walked. We had-- I had money. If some places we could get some food, we got. If not, we went to-- we begged. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we came to the cities. We worked. I got work in a factory, any place they wanted me, just to get to survive. And then, we came to Stalingrad. Is that--

speaker: interviewer
text: That is Stalinograd? Which is not the same as Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, there were rumors. Then maybe if he'll go, we can go through the borders and to get to Afghanistan, Iran, Iraq, and to get to Israel. But at that time, we were told already, you can't. Because they're shooting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's shooting?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Iran, Iraq, Afghanistan, any place. You cannot go there no more. So we decided-- we worked in Stalingrad. But soon, the Germans were close. We took-- my nephew didn't want to go no more. He said, it's no use to run. Hitler's gonna be all over. Why to suffer? Let him kill us and finish. [CRYING] I said, no. We have to go. [SIGHS] We took the last boat from Stalingrad on the Volga. And it was full with refugees. And the boat-- the boat moved already. My nephew was tall and jumped in. I cannot swim, but I let myself go under the Volga. And they felt sorry for me. And they took me out with a-- a rope.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Gordon?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was about two, three years younger than me. Was-- he was my oldest sister's son. And I was the baby. So it was a big difference. And the boat was full with people. So they took me out with a rope. Because I was running. I couldn't swim. But life was-- was-- you never-- I never thought that I'm gonna drown and I'm gonna-- just do what-- I-- I never even thought, I can do it. Anyways, they saved me. And from the boat, we came to Tashkent, far away from Hitler. I thought, well, now Hitler wouldn't get us. Maybe the war will be finished. We went to work in a kolkhoz. When I came to Stalingrad and I saw people dying and dying, lying, swollen from hunger, dirty, filthy, I said to myself, no. I'm not gonna lie down and die. Let's go to a farm. We'll work in a kibbutz. And they call it a kolkhoz. And we left to a kolkhoz, [UZBEK].

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, near Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: [UZBEK]

speaker: interviewer
text: [UZBEK]

speaker: interviewee
text: [UZBEK] And we worked there. And we heard then, the Germans are fighting already in Stalingrad, from door to door, from house to house. And I got swollen, working on the farm with a little bit of rice, being almost two years there and not have any vitamins. I thought I just got fat. But I got swollen. I didn't even know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the doctor. And I got swollen. I couldn't-- I couldn't eat. I got like a sore throat. So I thought it's only a cold. I came to a doctor from the kolkhoz. And he was a Russian, which they sent-- Russia sent him away. I don't know what he did. Like a-- who knows what he did. But he didn't come free like I came. And he said, well, I had the blight blisters in my throat. It's not a cold. I haven't got any vitamins in my body. I said, why don't you give me something? He said, if Hitler will win Stalingrad, you're gonna have your medicine. So I knew with whom I'm talking. So I said, how long can I live? The most, about six months. Did the people there know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, you knew that I'm Jewish. Yeah. He was a real Russian. I was Jewish. The people I worked in kolkhoz, I don't think they knew that I'm Jewish. Because most of the people were Korean people. I worked with Korean people. I worked-- worked with Uzbek-- Uzbekistan. I don't know if they knew. But I had to learn their language. Because they didn't know how to get in touch with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do on the kolkhoz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working on the rice. I plant rice. And the rice, I plant in water. The water is running from the hills. The water was freezing. And I had to start work in the water up to my chest every single day. 6 o'clock in the morning, I had to be on the-- on the kolkhoz and to work on that. And if I didn't take my normal what I had to do, I couldn't get the little bit of rice what they gave me. If I couldn't finish, then there was times when you take off the-- the rice. You let out the water. You take out the rice. And it's very, very hot in Tashkent. And we could work only till 12 o'clock. 12 o'clock, everybody was hiding in the straw cabins. And I could not go to hide by 12 o'clock. And the lights were-- the heat was unbearable, because I couldn't finish the normal. So I had to work until I got the normal to get the little bit of rice. And that's why, after, I got swollen. When I came to the doctor, and he didn't give me any medicine, and I came back-- when I came back there, I worked with two girls from-- they were-- from Kharkov. Young girls my age, crying. Their mother and father passed away. Because they were elder people. They couldn't get-- they got sick, and they couldn't-- and they died. And they took their mother and their father with them. And they put him in a old blanket, put it under a hill, took a-- a grave. And nobody will even know where the grave is under a hill. And I came after.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the doctor didn't give me any medicine, I said, oh no. My body will not lie there where these people are lying. Nobody on earth will find out. But what can I do? I went to the office. I swore, I'll do anything possible. But my body's not gonna be there. But what can I do? They took away the passport. They took away anything. I couldn't move. What kind of passport did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a Polish passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took away them, which we couldn't leave. And I swore. And it reminds me, when I came to Canada, when I saw when she was on the hill swearing that she will be not dead. And that, I said to my children, you know, your mother was there. And I thought, I'll go to the office, and I'll beg them to give me the rice what I earned. And maybe I can go to Tashkent and find a job. But in the meantime, I saw they have a big advertising. They need 40 strong people. And they sending away with the-- to dig canals. And I thought to myself-- but I knew, there, they're giving you good food, because you have to work very hard. And I was young, but very fat and swollen. But they didn't know that I'm swollen. And they said, that, gee whiz, that's a good young girl that will work good on the farm there-- on the canal. They sent me away for a month. And they had no right to take you back from there before a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Gordon work on the kolkhoz with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gordon was in the same-- but they sent him in another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he worked also on the rice with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. And when I came there, they took us on a wagon. I didn't have nothing. I had a coat from home, something. So they dig in the ground, like an office, and closed up on the top with straw. And we had to work. And there was a hill. You had-- they put earth on your back. You had to go on the hill, up to the hill, throw out the earth, and come back. It was 40 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this place? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and if you go slow, all the people are behind you. But they gave us good food. They got us meat and soup and rice and bread. And eat as much as you can. It was heaven. But you have to work. I tried my best, because my feet were swollen. But I-- with all my might, I did it. I worked two days. And on the third day, they called me to the office. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was, I believe, a-- a major, a very-- a Russian major with all his--

speaker: interviewer
text: Medals.

speaker: interviewee
text: --medals. Handsome and tall. And he started to talk to me. Then, he's going to send me to Siberia. I'm such a healthy young girl. I don't want to work. I slow down. And all the 40 people are behind me. And he was talking and talking. And I got speechless. I couldn't answer. And he got mad. He got red. He talks and I cannot answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, hysterical, I open my mouth, with such a crying. Then, he got scared. And he came, patted me on my shoulder, said-- in Russian, he said, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. It means, quiet down, quiet down. Finally, when I quiet down, with tears in my eyes, I told him all the story. Why do I have to die? What did I do to die? Because I was born Jewish? I said, no. I'm not gonna die. And you're not going to send me to Siberia. For what? I didn't do nothing wrong. And he felt sorry for me. And he said, what do you want me to do? I said, yes. There are these people working there to mark how many through the [RUSSIAN]. Like, how much-- How many working days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Working days and how much you did. And these, they're working in the office. I was very good in math in school. I said, give me work in the office. And you'll see that I'll do more than the girls who are sitting there. Try me. He didn't have any choice. He cannot send me back. He gave me a scribbler and a pencil and told me to work and to go out in the field and to check how many they dug out. And every time, I couldn't believe that I have nothing on my back, and it's so easy. It's just a miracle happened. And-- and I ate every day. I did my work in the office. I did the girls' work. They gave me their bread. They gave me everything. Because they weren't hungry like me. And every single day, I got thinner and thinner. All the swelling went down. And I said, I'm not gonna die. These kept me not-- not to die, only aiming that I'll come to Canada to see my sisters. After the month, I came back to the kolkhoz. I have shoes from home. I couldn't wear them still. A dress what I couldn't wear. I looked just like I came from home. Now, I have strength again, hopefully till the war will be finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. We need to stop here, Sonia. We'll continue on the next tape. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.
